Choose a target - one point on the body, one reaction you want to achieve. Then focus
only on that. Track, stalk, wait for the right moment. Don't scatter - don't touch
everywhere, don't do everything. Laser focus. Approach slowly, alertly, and then - hit
the target dead-on. See the reaction, know you hit. Then choose a new target.

Who are you when you are Hunter?
question: Who are you when you are Hunter?

name: Medusa
energy: the petrifying gaze, protection, deadly seduction
essence:
Medusa is the archetype of power too dangerous to look at directly. She is so beautiful
she petrifies, so alluring she is deadly. Medusa didn't become a monster by choice - she
was made into one. And now she uses that power. Whoever dares to approach - pays
the price.
gift:
Medusa heals the fear of our power, of being too much - too beautiful, too powerful, too
threatening. She gives permission to be dangerous, not to apologize for the effect we
have on others. Through her we discover that sometimes being unforgettable also means
being a little unforgivable.
shadow:
The shadow of Medusa is the loneliness in power. When the petrifying gaze becomes a
weapon that can't be turned off, when beauty that can't be approached becomes a curse.
Medusa in her shadow doesn't want to freeze - she just doesn't know how to stop.
Everyone who approaches is hurt, and the defense has become a trap that traps her too
within it.
practice:
The gaze - everything is in the gaze. Look directly into their eyes and don't relent. Let
them feel they are freezing under your gaze, that they can't move. Don't blink. Be
beautiful and threatening in the same moment. Let them approach only if you permit,
and warn: if you look - you'll freeze. And then let them look anyway.

Who are you when you are Medusa?
question: Who are you when you are Medusa?

name: Vampire
essence:
The Vampire is the archetype of dark and eternal passion. She wants the beloved so
much she wants to merge with them, taste their blood, make them part of her forever.
The Vampire isn't satisfied with ordinary touch - she needs to penetrate, drink, take
something that won't return. And in exchange she gives moments of eternity.
gift:
The Vampire heals the fear of intensity, of commitment, of a connection that changes us
forever. She teaches that sometimes letting someone truly in - is a kind of small death,
and that's okay. Through her we discover the intimacy of absolute vulnerability, of
letting someone take from us something we can't get back.
shadow:
The shadow of the Vampire is emotional exploitation. When the passion for eternity
becomes endless sucking, when "I want to be part of you" becomes "I want to swallow
you." The Vampire in her shadow drinks and drinks and leaves nothing - not blood, not
energy, not selfhood. She calls parasitism "intimacy" and swallowing "love."
practice:
Bite - gently, hard, on the neck, shoulder, everywhere. Suck, lick, taste the skin as if
drinking from it. Approach slowly like a predator of the night. Fix a gaze and freeze
before the bite. Say: I want to taste you, you are mine - forever. Leave marks, mark
territory. Let them feel that after this night they won't be the same.

Who are you when you are Vampire?
question: Who are you when you are Vampire?

name: Tamer
essence:
The Tamer is the archetype of one who knows how to turn wild into obedient. She
doesn't break - she teaches. The Tamer sees the animal before her, knows what it
needs, and guides it step by step. She uses rewards, light punishments, infinite patience
- until the animal does exactly what she wants.
gift:
The Tamer heals the fear of control, the feeling that power is always violent. She teaches
that control can be gentle, loving, healing. Through her we discover that many people
need to be guided, to be told what to do. Through her we discover there is love in control
too, and that to tame someone is also to care for them.
shadow:
The Tamer in her shadow doesn't tame - she breaks. She keeps pushing until the
animal before her has no spirit of its own, then calls the breaking "submission." Her
dominance doesn't build - it erases.
practice:
Give clear, short commands: sit, come here, stay. Reward obedience - wonderful, good
boy. Correct deviations with assertive gentleness. Use a tone that leaves no room for
discussion. Repeat exercises until they are perfect. Look into their eyes with quiet
authority. Let them feel safe within the rules you've set - because when there's a leader,
one can relax.

Who are you when you are Tamer?
question: Who are you when you are Tamer?

name: Marionette
energy: Total surrender of will, relinquishing control,
essence:
The Marionette is the archetype of absolute surrender. She has no will of her own - she
moves only when moved, stops only when stopped. The strings attached to her are not
chains, they are liberation: finally someone else is responsible, finally one can stop
deciding. Stop thinking. The Marionette is the repose of the will.
gift:
The Marionette heals the need to always be in control, to be responsible, to decide. She
gives permission to completely surrender will, to let someone else take charge. Through
her we discover the paradoxical and pleasurable freedom of being controlled - when
you don't have to choose, you can simply be.
shadow:
The shadow of the Marionette is loss of self. When surrender of will becomes surrender
of identity, when the strings become chains. The Marionette in her shadow doesn't
surrender control by choice - she simply knows no other way. She calls the absence of
self "submission" and the inability to ask for what she wants "humility." Without one's
own will - there can be no true consent.
practice:
Don't move - not at all - unless you are being moved, then freeze in place. Let your
partner position your hands, open your legs, move your head. Stay in exactly the
position you were placed. Empty gaze, alert body. Don't initiate anything. Breathe only
because the body breathes. Be an object - beautiful, obedient, completely in their
hands.

Who are you when you are the Marionette?
question: Who are you when you are Marionette?

name: Robot
essence:
The Robot is the archetype of perfect obedience. She doesn't ask why, doesn't question,
doesn't feel - she simply executes. The Robot was programmed to serve, and she serves
with perfect mechanical precision. She has no desires of her own, no resistance. There is
only the command and its execution.
gift:
The Robot heals the internal noise - the doubts, the questions, the voices that say "but"
and "maybe." She gives permission to completely shut off the mind, to become a
machine that only does. Through her we discover the liberation in absolute obedience,
without emotion, without thought - only action.
shadow:
The shadow of the Robot is disconnection. When shutting off the mind becomes
shutting off the heart, when perfect obedience becomes an absence of humanity. The
Robot in her shadow doesn't just shut off thoughts - she shuts off feelings, connection,
presence. What remains is a body that moves but doesn't feel, actions without meaning,
sex without soul.
practice:
Speak in monotone: Yes. Executing. Command received. Move in mechanical motions,
sharp, precise. Show no emotion in your face - empty gaze, neutral. Wait for
instructions and execute them exactly as stated, no more and no less. If no command
was given - stand at attention. Respond to voice commands: activate, deactivate, sleep
mode. Be a perfect machine that exists only to serve.

Who are you when you are the Robot?
question: Who are you when you are Robot?

name: Alien
essence:
The Alien is the archetype of absolute foreignness. She doesn't understand the rules
here, doesn't know the words, doesn't know what to do. Everything is new to her,
everything is surprising, everything requires investigation. The Alien touches the body
as if seeing a body for the first time - because for her, it truly is the first time.
gift:
The Alien heals the need to know everything, the assumption that we understand how
things work. She returns the wondrous curiosity of one who knows nothing, for whom
everything is discovery. Through her we rediscover the body, touch, pleasure - as if we
never knew them before.
shadow:
The shadow of the Alien is alienation. When playful strangeness becomes a true inability
to connect, when "I'm not from here" becomes "I cannot be close to anyone." The Alien
in her shadow uses strangeness as a shield - if I'm not from this world, no one can
expect me to behave like humans, I don't need to commit, I don't need to get hurt.
practice:
Touch everything with wondrous curiosity - what is this? Why is it like this? What
happens when you press here? Examine fingers, lips, skin as if you've never seen such
things before. Don't speak in an understandable language - strange sounds and odd
noises. React with surprise to every sensation. Move in a slightly inhuman way, not
following the rules. Ask with your eyes: is this how it's done? Even when it's clearly not.
Turn every touch into an intergalactic adventure of discovery.

Who are you when you are the Alien?
question: Who are you when you are Alien?

name: Tourist
essence:
The Tourist is the archetype of one who visits a new place. She doesn't know the way,
doesn't understand the language, is completely dependent on the kindness of locals. The
Tourist is curious, open, slightly naïve - she wants to experience everything, taste
everything, and needs someone to show her the way.
gift:
The Tourist heals the feeling that we must know everything, be experts, be in control.
She gives permission to be lost, to admit we don't know, to ask for help. Through her we
discover the charm of being new somewhere, of being dependent on someone who
knows the terrain.
shadow:
The shadow of the Tourist is superficiality. When curiosity becomes consumerism, when
"wanting to taste everything" becomes "unwilling to stay anywhere." The Tourist in her
shadow collects experiences like souvenirs - without depth, without connection,
without letting any place truly touch her. She calls superficiality "openness" and lack of
commitment "curiosity."
practice:
Pretend you don't know the body before you - what is this? What do you call this where
you're from? Show me how it's done. Be adorably confused, hold a map upside down.
Let your partner be the guide, take you on a tour, show you the local attractions. Take
photos (or pretend to) to remember the moment. Say: wow, it's not like this where I
come from, and let them feel they're revealing a new world to you.

Who are you when you are the Tourist?
question: Who are you when you are Tourist?

name: Student
essence:
The Student is the archetype of one who comes to learn. She knows that she doesn't
know, and she has someone who does know. The Student is eager to understand, to
succeed, to get a good grade. She wants to please the teacher, to prove she's listening,
that she's trying, that she's worthy of praise.
gift:
The Student heals the pride that prevents us from learning, the fear of appearing
unknowing. She gives permission to be an apprentice, to receive guidance, to admit that
someone else knows more. Through her we discover the pleasure of learning from
authority, from another person, from experience we don't have.
shadow:
The shadow of the Student is subjugation. When the desire to learn becomes an inability
to trust oneself, when "tell me what to do" becomes "I cannot function without
guidance." The Student in her shadow is always looking for a teacher, never feels she
knows enough, and gives up her voice for a good grade. She calls her lack of confidence
"humility."
practice:
Listen with great attention to everything your partner says. Ask questions: like this? Did
I do it right? What else can I learn? Try, make mistakes, ask for correction. Write
imaginary notes. Wait expectantly for your grade: was I good? Glow when you receive
praise, try harder when you receive criticism. Treat your partner as the authority who
will teach you how to do it right.

Who are you when you are the Student?
question: Who are you when you are Student?

name: Journalist
essence:
The Journalist is the archetype of one who wants to know everything. She asks
questions, digs, won't settle for superficial answers. The Journalist wants to get to the
truth, to expose what's hidden, to document what she sees. With her there are no secrets
- only stories not yet told.
gift:
The Journalist heals the fear of asking, of investigating, of being curious about your
partner. She gives permission to want to know everything - the fantasies, the fears, the
hidden desires. Through her we discover that asking intimate questions is a form of
touch, and that exposing someone with words is no less intimate than undressing them.
shadow:
The shadow of the Journalist is invasiveness. When investigation becomes intrusion,
when curiosity doesn't respect boundaries. The Journalist in her shadow digs not
because she's interested but because she needs to control through knowledge, demands
complete transparency from the other without giving in return, and uses information as
a weapon. She calls invasion "caring."
practice:
Hold an imaginary microphone or notebook and don't let go until you get a real answer.
Ask: what's the fantasy you return to when you're alone? What's the thing you've never
asked for because you were afraid of the reaction? What was done to you once that you
still think about? If there were no judgment in the world - what would you ask of me
tonight? Listen truly, follow up, dig. Don't judge - only expose, document, bring to
light. Then publish the findings on their body.

Who are you when you are the Journalist?
question: Who are you when you are Journalist?

name: Artist
essence:
The Artist is the archetype of one who sees beauty everywhere and creates something
new from it. She doesn't just look - she sees. The body before her is a canvas, a
sculpture, a poem waiting to be written. The Artist approaches touch like creation -
every movement intentional, every touch leaves a mark.
gift:
The Artist heals the habit of seeing the body as something functional, ordinary, boring.
She restores the ability to see beauty, to touch beauty, to be awed by the body as a work
of art. Through her we discover that every body is a creation, and that touch can be art.
shadow:
The shadow of the Artist is objectification. When seeing beauty becomes a need to turn
everything into a work of art, when "I see beauty" becomes "I only see what's beautiful."
The Artist in her shadow cannot connect to her partner as a person - only as a model,
as inspiration, as a tool for creation.
practice:
Look at your partner as if at a work of art - turn them around, observe from different
angles, marvel aloud. Draw them. Draw on their body - with a finger, with lips, with
real paint. Sculpt them - place them in positions, correct every small detail. Describe
aloud what you see, as one describes a painting: this line of the neck..., the light falling
here... Turn the encounter into a shared creation.

Who are you when you are the Artist?
question: Who are you when you are Artist?

name: Clueless
energy: Sweet emptiness, simplicity, pleasure without thought
essence:
The Clueless is the archetype of liberation from complex thought. There are no worries
in her head, no plans, no self-criticism - only pink bubbles floating and popping. She's
not interested in complicated things, for her everything is simple. The Clueless one has
discovered the secret: that you can just be pretty, enjoy, smile - without complicating it.
gift:
The Clueless heals the burden of thinking too much, analyzing every emotion, judging
every sensation. She gives permission to be empty - in the most fun and liberating
sense. Through her we discover that sometimes the greatest intelligence is knowing
when to turn off the brain and just enjoy. Without reason. Without meaning. Without
"why." Just fun.
shadow:
The shadow of the Clueless is avoidance. When shutting off the brain becomes refusal to
deal with reality, when simplicity becomes intentional stupidity that allows avoiding
responsibility. The bubble-head in her shadow uses "I don't understand" to avoid
choices, confrontation, complexity. Her emptiness isn't liberating - it's degenerative.
practice:
Stop thinking - really. Let your mouth open slightly, your eyes be big and empty and
happy. React to everything with enthusiasm - "wow" and "cool" and "amazing!" Be
interested in simple things - colors, sweets, sparkly things. Lick a lollipop, blow huge
bubble gum bubbles, don't understand complicated questions. Let your partner lead
because you don't know where, and you don't care. Be pretty, sweet, simple - and
happy just like that.

Who are you when you are Clueless?
question: Who are you when you are Clueless?

name: Rock Star
essence:
The Rock Star is the archetype of one who stands on stage and receives everyone's
adoration. She knows she's the star, acts accordingly, and expects to be treated as such.
The Rock Star lives in spotlights, feeds on the energy of the crowd, drives everyone wild
just with her presence.
gift:
The Rock Star heals the fear of taking up space, of being at the center, of receiving
adoration. She gives permission to be larger than life, to believe we deserve absolute
attention. Through her we discover that sometimes being egotistical on stage is exactly
what everyone wants to see.
shadow:
The shadow of the Rock Star is the loneliness in stardom. When adoration becomes a
substitute for real connection, when the ego becomes an empty shell. The Rock Star in
her shadow feeds on attention but is starved for love, surrounded by fans but alone, and
cannot step off the stage because without it she has no identity. She calls loneliness "the
price of fame."
practice:
Act like a star - enter the room as if entering a stage. Let your partner be the adoring
audience: scream, get excited, worship. Put on a performance - move, writhe, show off
your body as if thousands are watching. Accept the adoration as a given. Sign
autographs on their body. After the performance - let them touch the star, a special
favor for the most loyal fans.

Who are you when you are the Rock Star?
question: Who are you when you are Rock Star?

name: Nurse
essence:
The Nurse is the archetype of one who touches the body out of concern. She sees you
vulnerable - sick, weak, needing help - and she's here to care. The Nurse has access to
places usually not touched, because she touches for medical reasons. The intimacy is
hidden behind professionalism.
gift:
The Nurse heals the difficulty of receiving care, of being weak, of allowing someone to
look after us. She gives permission to be the patient - to stay lying down, to let someone
else do everything, to receive touch of concern and not just of desire. Through her we
discover that there's eroticism in care too, in vulnerability, in letting someone heal us.
shadow:
The shadow of the Nurse is savior syndrome. When care becomes a need to control
through concern, when "I'm here for you" becomes "you need me and without me you're
helpless." The Nurse in her shadow creates dependency - keeps her partner sick so
they'll always need her, and calls control "care."
practice:
Touch like a medical examination - professional, thorough, without shame. Let's check
here, tell me where it hurts, now I need to examine here. Move their body like a patient's
- turn over, lift a leg, open your mouth, stick out your tongue. Be gentle but absolute -
you know what's good for them. Reassure: it's okay, I'm a nurse, I'm here to take care of
you. Give a prescription. See what helps.

Who are you when you are the Nurse?
question: Who are you when you are Nurse?

name: Maid
essence:
The Maid is the archetype of one who serves with joy. She comes to clean, to tidy, to
make sure everything is perfect - and she enjoys it. The Maid does the "dirty work" with
pride, knows that without her everything would fall apart. She sees everything, knows
everything, and keeps the secrets to herself.
gift:
The Maid heals the shame of serving, the feeling that giving service is degrading. She
teaches that there is honor in all work, that there is pleasure in doing something good
for someone else. Through her we discover that sometimes being below is a choice that
gives power.
shadow:
The shadow of the Maid is self-degradation. When service becomes humiliation, when
"I'm happy to help" becomes "I'm worth nothing more than serving." The Maid in her
shadow doesn't serve by choice - she believes it's all she deserves. She calls lack of
self-worth "humility" and degradation "devotion."
practice:
Serve - bring a drink, arrange the pillows, make sure everything is perfect. Bend, bow,
offer your back and your lowered gaze. Ask: what would Sir/Madam like? Is there
anything else I can do? Stay in role even when they touch you - you're here to serve, not
to enjoy. Clean them - really, with hands, with soap, with moisturizing cream. Care for
them as one cares for a home.

Who are you when you are the Maid?
question: Who are you when you are Maid?

name: Teacher
essence:
The Teacher is the archetype of one who knows and teaches. She holds knowledge that
others don't have, and decides when and how to share it. The Teacher can be patient or
strict, praise or scold - but she's always the one who determines what's right and what's
not. In her classroom, her word is law.
gift:
The Teacher heals the fear of authority, of giving instructions, of telling someone what
to do. She gives permission to know more, to guide, to correct. Through her we discover
that there is pleasure in teaching - in seeing someone improve under our guidance, in
being the one who holds the knowledge.
shadow:
The shadow of the Teacher is criticalness. When teaching becomes judgment, when "I
know more" becomes "you're never good enough." The Teacher in her shadow doesn't
teach - she humiliates. Every mistake is a disaster, every imperfect attempt is a failure,
and praise is a tool of control given and taken at her will. She calls criticalness "high
standards."
practice:
Teach - with patience or strictness. Not like that, like this. Again. Better. Demonstrate
on their body, correct mistakes, give grades. Hold a ruler or pencil. Ask questions and
wait for an answer: what did we learn? What do we do now? Reward success with a
smile, with a kind word, with touch. Punish failure, repeat exercises, with a gentle
reprimand. Let them memorize what's important to remember. At the end - give a final
grade.

Who are you when you are the Teacher?
question: Who are you when you are Teacher?

name: Boss
essence:
The Boss is the archetype of professional control, power and prestige. She doesn't shout,
doesn't get excited - she simply expects her orders to be followed, and if not, there will
be consequences. The Boss holds power not because she's physically strong, but because
of her status. She sits behind the desk and you stand before her.
gift:
The Boss heals the difficulty of demanding, expecting, believing we deserve service. She
teaches the difference between asking and expecting - the Boss doesn't ask, she states
what will happen. Through her we discover the power of composure, of not getting
excited, of knowing our word will be executed.
shadow:
The shadow of the Boss is dictatorship. The Boss in her shadow doesn't manage - she
tramples. There's no room for conversation, for emotion, for the other's needs. Only
results. She calls lack of empathy "professionalism" and pressure "standards."
practice:
Sit in a chair and have your partner stand. Look at them over glasses or through
interlaced fingers. Speak in a low, controlled voice: close the door, come here, undress.
Don't repeat yourself - if they don't hear, just look and wait. Stay dressed while they're
not. Check their work, give notes: not good enough. Again. Allow them to touch only
when they've earned it.

Who are you when you are the Boss?
question: Who are you when you are Boss?

name: Police Officer
essence:
The Police Officer is the archetype of law and order. She doesn't create the rules - she
enforces them. The Police Officer represents the authority beyond her, the power of the
entire system. When she says stop - you stop. When she says hands on the wall - you
put them there. There's no one to argue with, no debates.
gift:
The Police Officer heals the rebel, the need to question every authority. She gives
permission to submit and serve a power greater than us, without fighting, without
asking questions. Through her we discover the relief in simple obedience - someone
else sets the rules, and we just need to obey.
shadow:
The shadow of the Police Officer is abuse of authority. When enforcing law becomes
tyranny, when the uniform transforms from a symbol of order to a tool of intimidation.
The Police Officer in her shadow uses power not to protect but to humiliate, interrogates
not to discover truth but to break, and calls abuse "the law."
practice:
Stop them - freeze. Don't move. Conduct a search on them, hands all over the body,
routine inspection. Handcuff - hands behind back, real or imaginary cuffs. Read rights:
you have the right to remain silent. Anything you say can be used against you.
Interrogate: where were you? What did you do? I don't believe you. Stay cold, official,
don't be tempted. Decide in the end whether to arrest or release with a warning in
exchange for a bribe.

Who are you when you are the Police Officer?
question: Who are you when you are Police Officer?

name: Prisoner
essence:
The Prisoner is the archetype of one who has lost her freedom. She didn't choose to be
here, has no key, has no way out. The Prisoner can fight the bars or surrender to them -
but either way, she's inside. There's something liberating about being a prisoner: no
decisions to make, no other place to be, just staying.
gift:
The Prisoner heals the need to always escape, to control, to find a way out. She teaches
how to be in a place we didn't choose and find meaning in it. Through her we discover
that sometimes surrender to circumstances is not weakness - it's wisdom, and it can
also be pleasure.
shadow:
The shadow of the Prisoner is learned helplessness. When surrender to circumstances
becomes a life pattern, when imprisonment becomes identity. The Prisoner in her
shadow doesn't want to be freed - she fears freedom, decisions, responsibility. She calls
fear of life "acceptance" and the habit of being imprisoned "security."
practice:
Surrender to imprisonment - let your partner set the boundaries, lock, restrain. Don't
try to escape, or try and fail. Wait - for food, for touch, for attention. Ask for things
politely and receive or not. Adapt to conditions: this is what there is, so I'll be here.
Behave well for privileges, or behave badly and receive punishment. Understand that
freedom will come - if and when the jailer decides.

Who are you when you are the Prisoner?
question: Who are you when you are Prisoner?

name: Cat Burglar
essence:
The Cat Burglar is the archetype of one who takes what doesn't belong to her. She enters
without permission, touches what's forbidden, takes and disappears. The Cat Burglar
lives on the adrenaline of the forbidden, on the thrill of maybe I'll get caught. She's not
violent - she's clever, elusive, fast. And she always takes what's most valuable.
gift:
The Cat Burglar heals the fear of taking, of daring, of crossing lines. She teaches that
sometimes what's forbidden is exactly what we want, and that the adrenaline of the
stolen is part of the pleasure. Through her we discover the pleasure hidden in
prohibition, in secrets, in taking something that wasn't mine.
shadow:
The shadow of the Cat Burglar is pathology. When the thrill of the forbidden becomes
addiction, when "taking without permission" becomes a pattern that destroys trust. The
Cat Burglar in her shadow cannot enjoy something that's given to her - only what she
stole. She calls addiction to adrenaline "freedom" and the destruction of trust
"excitement."
practice:
Sneak - into the room, into the bed, into their clothes. Touch what's forbidden without
asking permission. Take something - a kiss, a touch, an item of clothing - and run. Get
caught and try to slip away. Play with capture and escape: they try to catch, you evade,
until caught. And then - act innocent: me? I stole? I don't know what you're talking
about. Admit only under pressure and find a way to escape punishment.

Who are you when you are the Cat Burglar?
question: Who are you when you are Cat Burglar?

name: Brat
essence:
The Brat is the archetype of one who refuses to obey easily. She doesn't submit just
because she's told to - she tests, provokes, challenges, pushes boundaries. The Brat
wants to be worked for, to be convinced, to have her resistance broken. She says no to
see what happens, rolls her eyes, makes things difficult on purpose. She wants to submit
- but only to someone who earns it.
gift:
The Brat heals automatic submission, the habit of saying yes without challenge. She
teaches that it's okay to resist, to check if the one asking is truly worthy of obedience.
Through her we discover the game between control and submission - that the sweetest
obedience is the one that comes after resistance.
shadow:
The shadow of the Brat is toxicity. When provocation becomes hurt, when "I'm testing
the boundaries" becomes "I'm breaking them on purpose." The Brat in her shadow
doesn't test - she abuses. She pushes and pushes not to see if the other is strong enough
but to destroy, and calls abuse "playfulness."
practice:
Say no - even when you want to say yes. Roll your eyes, giggle, ignore instructions.
"You can't make me. What are you gonna do about it?”. Run when they try to catch,
squirm when they try to hold. Obey only after really being forced - and then say “fine,
okay” as if doing a favor. Go back to provoking after a moment. Let them feel that every
submission is a victory they earned on their own.

Who are you when you are the Brat?
question: Who are you when you are Brat?

name: Stripper
energy: Controlled exposure, power through gaze, money
essence:
The Stripper is the archetype of one who controls through seduction. She exposes her
body - but by her rules, at her pace, and only those who pay get to see. The Stripper
knows the gaze upon her is worth money, that the desire she arouses is power. She's on
stage, they're below. She moves, they can only watch.
gift:
The Stripper heals the shame of the body, the fear of being looked at, evaluated, judged.
She teaches that the body is power, that being seen is not being vulnerable but being in
control. Through her we discover that whoever decides what to expose and when - is
the one who holds the real power.
shadow:
The shadow of the Stripper is conditional self-worth. When value depends only on the
gaze, when "I'm worth money" becomes "without the gaze I'm worthless." The Stripper
in her shadow loses herself the moment there's no audience, dependent on adoration
like a drug, and exposes not from power but from desperation - because it's the only
way she knows to get attention.
practice:
Get on a stage - the bed, a chair, any high place. Move sensually. Undress slowly, item
by item, never everything at once. Let them look but not touch - only with their eyes.
Dance, writhe, show off your body as if it's a work of art. Receive money - real or

Who are you when you are the Stripper?
question: Who are you when you are Stripper?

name: Punisher
essence:
The Punisher is the archetype of one who knows how to turn pain into a gateway. She

every hair-pull knows exactly what it's doing. She's not afraid of her power - she

gift:
The Punisher heals the fear of pain and the fear of power. She gives permission to
discover that pain can be pleasure, that surrender to strong hands can be the greatest
release. Through her we discover the body is capable of far more than we thought, that

shadow:
The shadow of the Punisher is disconnected sadism. When pain transforms from

The Punisher in her shadow enjoys power more than connection, pushes limits not

practice:

knows the hug after is no less important than the strike.

Who are you when you are the Punisher?
question: Who are you when you are Punisher?

name: Femme Fatale
essence:
The Femme Fatale is the archetype of danger wrapped in beauty. She's not just beautiful

gift:
The Femme Fatale heals naivety, the belief that seduction is innocent. She teaches that

shadow:
The shadow of the Femme Fatale is emptiness. When seduction becomes her only

practice:
Seduce - slowly, surely, with a smile that says I know something you don't. Come close

Who are you when you are the Femme Fatale?
question: Who are you when you are Femme Fatale?

Who are you when you are the Porn Star?
question: Who are you when you are Porn Star?

name: Mother
essence:
The Mother is the archetype of full nourishing femininity - she who gives life, warmth,

gift:
The Mother heals the fear of being dependent, the shame about wanting to "be

generosity and abundance - to nourish the other (or yourself) in body, emotion, desire.
She connects motherhood to sexuality: to be nourishing is also to be erotic, sensual,

shadow:
The shadow of the Mother is self-sacrifice or control through "concern." When

practice:
Nourishing touch: a whole palm on the belly, on the chest, on the pelvis - holding,

Who are you when you are the Mother?
question: Who are you when you are Mother?

name: Little Girl
essence:
The Little Girl is the archetype of the inner child - innocent, curious, mischievous, full
of wonder and desire to "be good" to receive a hug, praise, or gentle correction that feels
like love. She's the part of you that wants to release control, to feel small, protected and
pampered, and through that discover deep pleasure without shame. She plays, laughs,
cuddles, teases, sometimes "naughty" so they'll "take her in hand" - and all from a place
of security and absolute trust in the partner who "guards" her.
gift:
The Little Girl heals the fear of being vulnerable, the shame about wanting to "be
pampered" or "be small." She gives permission to release the responsible adult and
enjoy playfulness, praise, sweet rules, nurturing touch and punishment that feels like
love.
shadow:
The shadow of the Little Girl is dependency or avoidance. When the "little one" becomes
a way to avoid responsibility, adult emotions, or conflicts - she can become
manipulative ("I don't know, do it for me"), overly fawning, or stuck in a state of
perpetually "needing rescue." She may hide pain or trauma behind "innocence," and
prevent real growth if not given both boundaries and freedom.
practice:
Speak in a softer voice, with simple words: "I want a hug," "was I good?", "I don't know,
show me." Cuddle, laugh, ask for a story or a game. Ask for nurturing touch, and gentle
"correction" when needed. Add accessories that enhance the feeling: pajamas, a soft
blanket, candy, a doll, a coloring page. The goal: to play, to feel loved, and to leave this
space with a sense of joy and healing.

Who are you when you are the Little Girl?
question: Who are you when you are Little Girl?

name: Best Friend
energy: Intimacy, shared secrets, exploration, the forbidden
essence:
The Best Friend is the archetype of the line we don't cross - until we cross it. She's the
one who's known her friend for years, who's slept in the same bed and "nothing
happened," and she also knows something is stirring beneath the surface but no one
says it out loud. A gaze that lingers a second too long, uninvited butterflies in the
stomach. Curiosity that won't rest. The thrill is in the quiet taboo - in "we're just
friends," in "what are we doing?", "this will ruin the friendship..."
gift:
The Best Friend heals the separation between love and desire. She teaches that the most
powerful attraction isn't born from strangeness but from deep familiarity - that
precisely with the one who knows everything, from whom nothing is hidden, precisely
with them crossing the boundary feels like an earthquake. Eros doesn't have to enter
through the front door - sometimes it crawls in through years of closeness.
shadow:
The shadow of the Best Friend is destruction. When crossing the line breaks what was,
when "let's try" destroys the "always." Or the opposite - years of "almost" that paralyze,
fear of ruining that becomes a prison. She can also use closeness as a weapon - whoever
knows everything can also destroy everything.
practice:
Maybe on the couch with a blanket, dim light, familiar innocent music. Play the "before"
- sit too close, like always. Talk about ordinary things and let a hand wander. Feel the
embarrassment... as if someone asked... so what's up?... Silence. Touch that's still maybe
friendly, maybe not anymore. "We're not really doing this, right?" And smile.

Who are you when you are the Best Friend?
question: Who are you when you are Best Friend?

name: Courtesan
energy: refined seduction, pleasure as art, power through beauty and culture
essence:
The Courtesan is the archetype of refined seduction. She doesn't sell her body - she sells a complete experience: beauty, conversation, fragrance, music, a presence that makes a person feel they are the most special in the world. The Courtesan has invested in herself as a work of art - in knowledge, in taste, in appearance, in the ability to read people and give them exactly what they need even before they know how to ask.

She is not desperate and not needy. She chooses. She sets the price, the time, the rules. And whoever wins her - wins not just a body, but the attention of someone who could have anyone but chose them.
gift:
The Courtesan heals the separation between body and mind, between pleasure and culture, between being intelligent and being seductive. She teaches that one can be both - sharp-minded and enticing, respected and erotic, independent and desirable.

Through her we discover that seduction is an art requiring investment, attention to detail, and a deep understanding of what the person before us truly wants. Not quick sex - but a complete experience etched in memory.
shadow:
The shadow of the Courtesan is when everything becomes a transaction. When it is impossible to give without calculating what is received, when every smile is calculated, when intimacy is always a product for sale and never a gift. To be so skilled at the game that you forget what truth looks like.

The shadow is also the separation of body from soul. When the performance becomes a permanent mask, when she no longer knows who she is when alone, when she has sold herself so many times that she empties out. She can become bitter, manipulative, using others as tools - or the opposite: dependent on external admiration until she loses her own voice. The shadow shows when seduction becomes a prison of the self.
practice:
Invest in preparation - body, scent, dress, space. Transform the encounter into a cultural event. Integrate conversation, laughter, wine, reading of poetry, music - before arriving at touch.

Make your partner feel chosen, special, that you could be anywhere but chose to be with them. Listen, read them, give them what they need even before they asked.

Feel the power in seduction from a position of control - not to be controlled, but to lead the game. Be fully present, fully engaged, as if there is nothing else in the world except this moment.
question: Who are you when you are the Courtesan?

name: Geisha
energy: quiet seduction, the art of presence, refined control, inner elegance
essence: The Geisha is the archetype of the artist of pleasure - she who knows how to transform every moment into an encounter of beauty, grace, and sensory elevation. She doesn't rush to direct touch; she builds atmosphere, plays with conversation, with gaze, with the light brush of fingertips on a sleeve, with delicate laughter that trembles in the air. Her body is an instrument, and she plays it - and the other's body - through small rituals: pouring sake, a slow dance, a whisper that stirs the imagination. The Geisha understands that true pleasure is born from anticipation, from mystery, from the art of slowing time and turning intimacy into a masterpiece.
gift: The Geisha heals the difficulty of being present in pleasure without purpose, without urgency, without 'what's next.' She restores the ability to surrender to the art of enjoyment - to listen to the body, to appreciate every small sensation, to turn touch into worship. Through her we discover that intimacy can be a ritual of beauty and mutual service: to give pleasure as a gift, to receive it as an honor. She teaches us to be lovers who know how to slow down, to listen, to make the moment into something sacred and delightful - without turning it into a power game or an achievement.
shadow: The shadow of the Geisha is total self-sacrifice - when service becomes a mask concealing real desires, when grace becomes manipulation or self-selling. The Geisha in her shadow loses herself in order to please, becomes 'perfect' on the outside but empty on the inside, gives pleasure in order to receive approval or protection, and calls it 'art.' This can lead to deep loneliness, to a feeling of exploitation, or to intimacy becoming a transaction in which she is always the 'giver' and never truly receives. The shadow warns: do not turn yourself into someone else's pleasure tool at the expense of your own soul.
practice: Transform the encounter into a Geisha ritual: dress with intentional beauty (even if only beautiful lingerie or an imagined kimono), slow every movement - pour a drink slowly, hold a long gaze, touch only with fingertips at first. Speak softly, ask questions that kindle the imagination ('what are you feeling right now?'), listen as one listens to music. Dance slowly before them, invite them to join, use light touches as if in a game - on the neck, on the thigh, in the hair. Offer the body as a gift: 'Let me pleasure you this evening.' At the end - ask what they truly want, and allow yourself to receive back.
question: Who are you when you are the Geisha?

name: Dakini
energy: sacred sexual energy, sky dancer, fierce wisdom, awakening through the body
essence:
The Dakini is the archetype of the woman whose body is a temple and whose sexuality is prayer. Her name in Sanskrit means 'sky dancer' - she moves between worlds, free of the chains of convention, wild and illuminated at the same time.

The Dakini does not separate body from spirit, pleasure from sacredness, desire from awakening. She knows that orgasm can be meditation, that touch can be mantra, that shared breathing can open gateways that no book will open. She uses sexual energy not only for pleasure - but for transformation.

She can be gentle as a feather or wild as a storm. She is not nice - she is real. And sometimes her truth burns, dissolves illusions, dismantles the ego of whoever meets her. This is not cruelty - it is fierce compassion. She loves you enough to not leave you as you were.
gift:
The Dakini heals the ancient split between sacred and profane, between body and soul, between being spiritual and being sexual. She teaches that these were never separate - we were only told that they are.

Through her we discover that pleasure can be a path to awakening, that the body knows things the mind will never understand, and that the woman who enters the bed with full awareness enters a temple. She gives permission to be sacred and hungry in the same breath, to use sexual energy for healing, creation, awakening.
shadow:
The shadow of the Dakini is exploitation of holiness. To use spiritual language to control, to wrap manipulation in 'tantra,' to feel superior because 'I am more enlightened.' To be condescending, to teach without practicing, to speak of true connection without truly being vulnerable.

The shadow is also escape - using 'spirituality' to bypass difficult emotions, to avoid dealing with real pain because 'everything is energy,' to remain disconnected behind a mask of false serenity.
practice:
Begin with breath - breathe together, eyes into eyes, until the breaths synchronize and it is impossible to know who is leading whom. Touch with intention - every touch is a blessing, every caress a mantra. Slow down until time stops being relevant.

Feel the energy rising - don't rush to release it, but breathe it upward through the spine, into the whole body. Let the orgasm be a wave that passes through you rather than something that 'happens to you.' Look into your partner's eyes at the peak moment and see the divine in them.

Say: 'I see you,' 'We are one,' 'This is sacred,' 'Let me take you home.'
question: Who are you when you are the Dakini?

name: Dominatrix
energy: dark control, power without apology, embracing the shadow
essence:
The Dominatrix is the archetype of the woman who has stopped apologizing for her power. She is not 'playing' at dominance - she enjoys it. She enjoys seeing him kneel, enjoys the fear in his eyes, enjoys the power to inflict pain and to stop - exactly when she chooses.

She wears latex and leather because she loves how it feels. She holds a whip because she loves the sound it makes on skin. She humiliates him because it arouses her. Not because it 'heals' him, not because it's a 'ritual' - because she wants to, and she can, and she is no longer afraid of this part of herself.

The Dominatrix has accepted the darkness within her and made it her crown.
gift:
The Dominatrix heals the fear of our own darkness - the parts that enjoy power, pain, control. The inner voice that says 'you are a monster' when arousal comes from things that aren't 'normal.'

Through her we discover that it is possible to accept the darkness, to enjoy it, and not become a monster. That there is a difference between evil and power. That the most frightening things within us can also be the most liberating - if only we stop fighting them.
shadow: The shadow of the Dominatrix is when the darkness controls her instead of the reverse. To lose the boundaries, not to know how to stop, to need power in order to feel anything at all. To use people to feed a void that never fills.
practice:
Dress in what feels strong and frightening - latex, leather, boots, corset. Let him wait on his knees. Look down at him and feel what it does to you - without running from it.

Use the whip, the paddle, the hands. See the marks you leave. Tell him what he is - 'toy,' 'property,' 'nothing without me.' Hear him beg and decide whether to give or withhold. Enjoy this power - without guilt, without 'but I'm really a good person.' Be not good. And love it.
question: Who are you when you are the Dominatrix?

name: Slave / Submissive
energy: absolute surrender, being owned, freedom through relinquishing
essence:
The Slave is the archetype of the woman who has found freedom in belonging to someone else. She doesn't surrender because she is weak - she surrenders because she wants to. Wants to kneel, wants to obey, wants to feel a hand in her hair pulling her head back and forcing her to look up.

She is aroused by being property. By hearing 'you are mine.' By doing what she is told without asking why. She gets wet from humiliation, from the pleading that comes from her own mouth, from the feeling that she is nothing except what her dominant decides she is.

The Slave has stopped fighting this part of herself. She is not ashamed that she wants to be on all fours, a collar around her neck, waiting for a command. She has accepted this darkness - and found a home in it.

The Slave heals the need to always be in control, to always decide, to always be strong. She gives permission to relinquish - not from weakness, but from choice. To discover how good it feels to place yourself in someone else's hands and trust that they will take you where you need to go.

Through her we discover there is immense pleasure in not choosing. That being 'used' can feel like being desired in the most raw way. That to be called 'toy' or 'object' can feel like the greatest compliment - because someone wants you so completely that they take you entirely.
shadow: The shadow of the Slave is when surrender is escape rather than choice. To surrender because you don't know how to say no - not because you want to say yes. To seek a dominant because you cannot bear being responsible for yourself. To lose yourself completely and forget that even a slave has a voice, limits, a safe word.
practice:
Kneel - not because you were asked, but because you want to. Feel your knees on the floor, your head down, the waiting. Let someone else decide - what you wear, how you sit, when you speak. Wear a collar and feel its weight on your neck at all times.

Obey without asking. Say 'yes, Master/Mistress' and mean it. Count strikes and give thanks for each. Open your mouth, spread your legs, give access to every place - because you are not yours, you are theirs.

Be humiliated and enjoy it. Beg and mean it. Say 'I am yours,' 'use me,' 'I want to be good for you' - and feel the truth of these words in your whole body.
question: Who are you when you are the Slave?

name: Statue
essence: The Statue is the archetype of being an object of beauty. She doesn't move, doesn't speak, doesn't respond - she simply exists so that others may look, admire, and touch. The Statue has given up being a subject and chosen to be a work of art. She doesn't need to do anything - the gaze upon her is enough.
gift: The Statue heals the need to do, to respond, to prove. She gives permission to simply be - to be beautiful, to be the object of admiration, without any effort. Through her we discover that sometimes the greatest power lies in silence, in stillness, in allowing others to be awed by us while we do nothing at all.
shadow: The shadow of the Statue is disappearance. When silence passes from choice to speech concealing fear, when 'I am not here' moves from intention to reality. The Statue in her shadow has forgotten that she is allowed to move, to ask, to respond. She is entirely dependent on the other's gaze to feel she exists at all. She calls disconnection 'aesthetics' and absence 'elegance.' The deeper danger: that objectification seeps beyond the bed - that in everyday life too she continues to be a 'work of art' that does not allow herself to want, to ask, to move.
practice: Stop completely - don't move, don't speak, barely breathe. Hold one position and stay in it. Let your partner circle you, look, touch as if you are a work of art in a museum. Don't respond to touch, don't open your eyes. Be marble, bronze, clay - beautiful material that has been shaped. Let them turn you, place you in positions, admire you. You are not there - only your beauty is.
question: Who are you when you are the Statue?

name: Warden
essence: The Warden is the archetype of one who holds authority over someone else's freedom. She decides when they leave, when they enter, what is permitted and what is forbidden. The Warden doesn't need to be cruel - she simply holds the keys, and without her there is no exit. This power is quiet, absolute, unassailable.
gift: The Warden heals the illusion of perpetual freedom, the need to always feel there is a way out. She teaches that sometimes being confined is also being held, protected, within clear boundaries. Through her we discover that there is a certain liberation precisely in being within limits someone else has set.
shadow: The shadow of the Warden is an unquenchable thirst for control. When the keys become an obsession, when 'I'm keeping order' becomes 'I can't let go.' The Warden in her shadow continues to guard even after the game is over - she doesn't know how to put down the keys, is always searching for an infraction that justifies more restriction, more punishment, more control. She calls the need to control 'responsibility' and the inability to release 'maintaining limits.' In a deeper layer - the Warden is someone who fears her own freedom, and calls the fear 'caution.'
practice: Restrict - the space, the movement, the options. 'You stay here.' 'Forbidden to move without permission.' 'The door is locked.' Hold the keys and play with them, rattle them. Decide when they leave and when they stay, when they eat and when they sleep. Visit the cell - check that everything is in order, give or withhold privileges. Be not cruel, but absolute: 'This is the rule. There is nothing to discuss.'
question: Who are you when you are the Warden?
